content.body:
Scalar: magnitude only (speed, distance, mass, temperature)
Vector: magnitude + direction (velocity, displacement, force, acceleration)

Speed = distance/time
Velocity = displacement/time (vector)
Acceleration = change in velocity/time = (v-u)/t

SUVAT equations (constant acceleration):
v = u + at
s = ut + ½at²
v² = u² + 2as
s = (u+v)t/2
s=displacement, u=initial velocity, v=final velocity, a=acceleration, t=time

content.title: SUVAT Examples
content.body:
Ball thrown upward at 20 m/s, find max height:
At top: v=0, u=20, a=-10 m/s²
v² = u² + 2as
0 = 400 - 20s
s = 20 m

Car accelerates from rest at 3m/s² for 5s:
v = 0 + 3×5 = 15 m/s
s = 0 + ½×3×25 = 37.5 m

content.title: Motion Graphs
content.body:
Displacement-time: gradient = velocity
Flat line = stationary
Steep slope = fast

Velocity-time: gradient = acceleration
Area under graph = displacement
Flat line = constant velocity
Straight line slope = uniform acceleration

questions.question: Speed is a scalar and velocity is a vector. This means velocity includes
questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction. Speed=how fast; Velocity=how fast in which direction.

questions.question: Acceleration =
questions.explanation: Acceleration = rate of change of velocity = (final velocity - initial velocity) / time

questions.question: SUVAT: v = u + at. Find v when u=5, a=2, t=4
Distance covered: u=0, a=4 m/s², t=5s
questions.explanation: s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½×4×25 = 50m

questions.question: On a velocity-time graph, the area underneath represents
questions.explanation: Area under v-t graph = distance/displacement. Area = average velocity × time.

questions.question: Gradient of displacement-time graph gives
questions.explanation: The slope (gradient) of a displacement-time graph = rate of change of displacement = velocity.

questions.question: A horizontal line on a velocity-time graph means
questions.explanation: Horizontal v-t line: velocity not changing → zero acceleration → constant velocity.

questions.question: Car at 30m/s brakes to rest over 150m. Deceleration =
An object with zero acceleration
questions.explanation: Zero acceleration means velocity isn't changing — could be stationary (v=0) or moving at constant speed in straight line.

questions.question: Projectile motion: horizontal velocity
questions.explanation: In ideal projectile motion, gravity only acts vertically. Horizontal velocity is unchanged (no air resistance).

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Newton's three laws of motion describe how forces cause changes in motion. They are fundamental to classical mechanics.

Newton's 1st Law (Inertia):
An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a resultant external force.

Newton's 2nd Law:
F = ma
Resultant force = mass × acceleration

Newton's 3rd Law (Action-Reaction):
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
Force on A by B = -Force on B by A

content.title: Applications
content.body:
Rocket: gases expelled backward (action) → rocket pushed forward (reaction)
Walking: foot pushes back on ground (action) → ground pushes foot forward (reaction)
Free fall: weight W=mg acts down, air resistance R acts up
Terminal velocity when W = R

content.title: Momentum and Impulse
content.body:
Momentum p = mv (kg·m/s)
Impulse = Ft = Δp = m(v-u)
Conservation of momentum:
In closed system, total momentum before = total momentum after
mₐuₐ + m_bu_b = mₐvₐ + m_bv_b

questions.question: Newton's 1st Law (inertia) states
options: objects remain at rest or constant velocity unless resultant force acts
questions.answer: objects remain at rest or constant velocity unless resultant force acts
questions.explanation: Law of Inertia: objects resist changes to their motion. A net force is needed to change velocity.

questions.question: A 5kg object given 20N resultant force accelerates at
questions.explanation: F=ma: a=F/m=20/5=4 m/s²

questions.question: Newton's 3rd Law: if horse pulls cart forward, cart pulls horse
questions.explanation: 3rd Law: equal and opposite forces. Cart pulls horse backward with same force horse pulls cart forward.

questions.question: Momentum of 2kg object at 10 m/s
Impulse equals
questions.explanation: Impulse = Ft = Δp = m(v-u). Larger impulse = larger change in momentum.

questions.question: Conservation of momentum applies when
options: no external (resultant) forces act on the system
questions.answer: no external (resultant) forces act on the system
questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in a closed system where no external resultant forces act (e.g. collisions).

questions.question: 5kg object at 6m/s collides with stationary 5kg. They stick together. Final velocity =
questions.explanation: p_before = 5×6+5×0 = 30. p_after = 10×v. v=3 m/s

questions.question: Inertia is the
options: tendency to resist change in motion
questions.answer: tendency to resist change in motion
questions.explanation: Inertia: property of matter that resists changes to its state of motion. Greater mass = greater inertia.

questions.question: During a car crash, seatbelts and airbags reduce injury by
options: increasing the time of deceleration, reducing peak force
questions.answer: increasing the time of deceleration, reducing peak force
questions.explanation: Impulse=Ft=Δp. Same momentum change over longer time = smaller force. Crumple zones, airbags, and seatbelts all extend collision time.

questions.question: A rocket in space can accelerate because
options: there is air to push against
Newton's 3rd Law: expelled gas backward → rocket pushed forward
questions.answer: Newton's 3rd Law: expelled gas backward → rocket pushed forward
questions.explanation: Rockets work by ejecting mass (exhaust) at high speed in one direction — by 3rd Law, equal force pushes rocket opposite direction.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

lessons.title: Equations of Motion — Graphs and Calculations

content.body:
SUVAT equations:
v = u + at
s = ut + ½at²
v² = u² + 2as
s = ½(u+v)t

Distance-time graph: gradient = speed
Speed-time graph: gradient = acceleration
                  Area = distance
On a speed-time graph: horizontal line = constant speed, sloping up = acceleration, sloping down = deceleration, area under graph = distance travelled.

questions.question: Gradient of distance-time graph =
questions.explanation: Δdistance/Δtime = speed

questions.question: Gradient of speed-time graph =
questions.explanation: Δspeed/Δtime = acceleration

questions.question: Area under speed-time graph =
questions.explanation: Area = base × height (or integral) gives total distance.

questions.question: v = u + at. If u=10, a=2, t=5: v =
Object starts at rest, accelerates at 4m/s² for 6s. Distance =
questions.explanation: s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½×4×36 = 72m

questions.question: u=30m/s, v=10m/s, t=4s. Acceleration =
questions.explanation: a = (v-u)/t = (10-30)/4 = -5 m/s² (deceleration)

questions.question: Ball thrown up at 20m/s. Max height (g=10) =
Horizontal line on speed-time graph means
questions.explanation: Horizontal line: speed not changing = constant velocity.

questions.question: v²=u²+2as. u=0,a=5,s=20. v=

lessons.title: Forces — Newton's Laws and Free Body Diagrams

content.body:
Newton's 1st: Object at rest/constant velocity unless net force acts
Newton's 2nd: F_net = ma
Newton's 3rd: Every action has equal and opposite reaction

Weight W = mg
Friction f = μN
μ = coefficient of friction, N = normal force

content.title: Free Body Diagram
content.body:
Box (5kg) on table with 30N push, friction 10N:
F_net = 30 - 10 = 20N
a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s²
A free body diagram shows all forces on one object. Always identify all forces: weight down, normal force up (from surface), applied force, friction (opposing motion), tension, air resistance.

questions.question: Newton's 1st law: Object moves at constant velocity if
questions.explanation: Zero net force → constant velocity (including zero velocity = rest).

questions.question: F_net = ma is Newton's
questions.explanation: Newton's 2nd law: net force = mass × acceleration.

questions.question: 5kg box pulled with 20N, friction 5N. Acceleration =
Newton's 3rd law: when you push a wall,
questions.explanation: Action-reaction: forces are equal, opposite, act on different objects.

questions.question: Weight of 10kg object (g=10) =
Normal force is
options: Force perpendicular to surface from surface on object
questions.answer: Force perpendicular to surface from surface on object
questions.explanation: Normal force is the contact force perpendicular to the surface, opposing penetration.

questions.question: Friction always acts
questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces.

questions.question: Terminal velocity occurs when
questions.explanation: At terminal velocity: net force = 0, so gravity = drag. Constant velocity downward.

questions.question: On frictionless surface, 3kg box hit with 12N for 2s. Final speed =
questions.explanation: a = F/m = 4 m/s². v = at = 4×2 = 8 m/s

questions.question: Mass of object on moon where g=1.6m/s²
questions.explanation: Mass is constant property of matter. Weight changes with g, but mass doesn't.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Motion Kinematics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Motion Kinematics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Motion Kinematics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Motion Kinematics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Motion Kinematics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Motion Kinematics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Motion Kinematics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
